briefing.headline: 1 high-priority item in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: St Emlyn's, AJEM, REBEL EM. Main themes: trials, general emergency medicine, evidence synthesis. Best early reads: To squeeze or not to squeeze. The ARISE-FLUIDS trial.
briefing.editorialHeadline: Fluid Wars, Late Strokes, and Streamlining ED Care
briefing.editorialSummary:
The data on septic shock management continues to temper enthusiasm for maximal intervention. The ARISE-FLUIDS trial results are a stark reminder that aggressive initial fluid loading combined with early vasopressors did not confer a survival benefit over more conservative strategies, urging us to approach resuscitation titration with genuine caution.

Shifting focus across the spectrum of acute care, we see utility in risk stratification tools. For syncope workups, the ROMEO score offers a practical way to safely de-escalate unnecessary echocardiograms when the predictive value is high for low-risk patients. Similarly, for PE management, low-risk patients (sPESI 0 or 1) may not require hospitalization for 30-day survival benefit, suggesting pathways ripe for outpatient optimization.

Beyond individual algorithms, systemic improvements are emerging: dedicated ED-ICU zones show promise in reducing overall hospital length of stay. Furthermore, while late-window stroke remains challenging, meta-analyses suggest that when EVT is applied judiciously to highly selected patients, salvageable benefit might still exist. Today’s reading set demands we balance the allure of aggressive intervention with emerging evidence favoring targeted, resource-conscious care.

dailyEditorial.title: To squeeze or not to squeeze. The ARISE-FLUIDS trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: The ARISE-FLUIDS data suggest caution regarding the routine implementation of both early vasopressors and high-volume fluids simultaneously in septic shock. When managing a patient in septic shock, remember that aggressive initial fluid resuscitation combined with immediate vasopressor support did not confer a survival benefit over more restricted fluid strategies. Always consider the underlying pathophysiology when titrating these interventions.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review discusses the ARISE-FLUIDS trial, which directly compared two resuscitation strategies in septic shock patients presenting to the emergency department: one group received early vasopressors alongside restric…
dailyEditorial.summary: This review discusses the ARISE-FLUIDS trial, which directly compared two resuscitation strategies in septic shock patients presenting to the emergency department: one group received early vasopressors alongside restricted fluid administration, while the other received higher fluid volumes with later initiation of vasopressors. The primary finding reported is that neither management strategy resulted in a statistically greater number of days alive at day 90 when compared head-to-head. This suggests that current resuscitation guidelines advocating for aggressive initial fluid loading combined with early vasopressor use may lack definitive superiority over more conservative approaches.

dailyEditorial.title: Endovascular intervention for acute stroke in the very late window: A meta-analysis of 90-day mRS and procedural outcomes
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For patients presenting significantly outside the standard time window, consider EVT only after rigorous selection criteria are met, as the benefit appears restricted to specific subgroups. Remember that while improved functional outcomes and reduced mortality were suggested, the overall evidence base remains somewhat heterogeneous across outcome measures. Proceed with caution and ensure comprehensive imaging supports intervention in these challenging late-window cases.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This meta-analysis synthesized data regarding the use of endovascular therapy (EVT) for acute ischemic stroke occurring in very late windows, specifically beyond the initial 24 hours.
dailyEditorial.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized data regarding the use of endovascular therapy (EVT) for acute ischemic stroke occurring in very late windows, specifically beyond the initial 24 hours. The authors concluded that when applied judiciously to selected patients, EVT can indeed offer benefits by improving functional outcomes and reducing all-cause mortality. However, the analysis also stressed that patient selection is paramount, suggesting that the benefit is not universal across all late-presenting stroke cohorts. Overall, while the potential for salvage remains, clinicians must interpret these findings with caution due to the mixed nature of the underlying data.

dailyEditorial.title: Rocuronium vs Succinylcholine for RSI: Awareness, Paralysis, and Post-Intubation Sedation
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When choosing a paralytic for RSI, remember that while rocuronium might theoretically delay post-intubation sedation compared to succinylcholine, the magnitude of this difference is clinically debatable. Regardless of agent choice, always plan ahead for adequate analgesia and sedation *before* intubating, as maintaining optimal patient comfort immediately post-RSI remains paramount.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review summarizes a retrospective study comparing the use of rocuronium versus succinylcholine during Rapid Sequence Intubation (RSI) in the emergency department setting, focusing on post-intubation sedation and an…
dailyEditorial.summary: This review summarizes a retrospective study comparing the use of rocuronium versus succinylcholine during Rapid Sequence Intubation (RSI) in the emergency department setting, focusing on post-intubation sedation and analgesia needs. The authors noted that while rocuronium appeared associated with delayed initiation of post-intubation sedation and analgesia compared to succinylcholine, they cautioned that the absolute median differences observed were quite small, casting doubt on definitive clinical significance. A key concern raised is the potential for awareness during paralysis when utilizing longer-acting agents like rocuronium. Given these nuances, the authors recommend proactively planning for adequate post-intubation sedation and analgesia before proceeding with RSI, particularly when using agents with prolonged effects.

dailyEditorial.title: Association of Serum Lactate with Outcome After Pediatric Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Secondary Analysis of the Therapeutic Hypothermia After Pediatric Cardiac Arrest (THAPCA) Trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If you encounter a child who has experienced OHCA and achieves ROSC, consider obtaining serial lactate measurements within the first six hours as part of your risk stratification. Higher levels are associated with worse one-year outcomes, suggesting it could guide more aggressive post-resuscitation care or further workup. However, this finding is based on an observational secondary analysis, so interpret these results cautiously.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This secondary analysis re-examined the THAPCA-OH trial data to specifically assess the utility of serum lactate levels in predicting outcomes following pediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA).
dailyEditorial.summary: This secondary analysis re-examined the THAPCA-OH trial data to specifically assess the utility of serum lactate levels in predicting outcomes following pediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). The key finding reported is a correlation between elevated lactate concentrations measured within the first six hours after return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) and poorer one-year survival. This suggests that lactate may serve as an adjunct biomarker for early neuroprognostication and overall mortality risk in this critically ill pediatric population. While the association was noted, it's important to remember this is a secondary analysis drawing conclusions from existing trial data.

dailyEditorial.title: Critical care delivery models in emergency departments: a systematic review of the literature and meta-analysis of related outcome effects
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing critically ill patients in the ED, structuring care within a dedicated critical care area like an ED-ICU appears beneficial based on this meta-analysis. You should consider advocating for or implementing such a model if your department lacks one, as it may help reduce both ICU transfers and overall hospital stay duration. Remember that this is a synthesis of existing data, so local resource limitations must guide implementation.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesized the current literature on various organizational models for delivering critical care within the emergency department setting, culminating in a meta-analysis of related outcomes.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review synthesized the current literature on various organizational models for delivering critical care within the emergency department setting, culminating in a meta-analysis of related outcomes. The authors categorized several approaches, with dedicated critical care areas, such as an ED-ICU setup, being the most frequently reported model. The quantitative analysis provides supportive evidence suggesting that establishing these specialized, dedicated critical care zones within the ED environment may confer benefits. Specifically, the findings indicate a potential reduction in both subsequent ICU admission rates and overall hospital length of stay for patients presenting to the ED with critically ill status when compared against other established delivery models. This synthesis helps clarify which structural approaches are most supported by current evidence.

dailyEditorial.title: Predicting Echocardiography Findings in Adults Presenting to the Emergency Department With Syncope: An External Validation of the ROMEO Score
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If you have a patient presenting with syncope and are considering an echo, remember that a zero ROMEO score suggests a very low probability of needing significant cardiac workup on TTE. You can use this tool to safely de-escalate testing for low-risk patients, reserving the echo resources for those who might benefit most from the study. However, always keep in mind that this is a predictive model and clinical judgment remains paramount.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This paper presents an external validation of the ROMEO score, a tool designed to predict whether a patient presenting with syncope will have significant findings on a Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE).
dailyEditorial.summary: This paper presents an external validation of the ROMEO score, a tool designed to predict whether a patient presenting with syncope will have significant findings on a Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE). The authors found that the score demonstrates robust predictive performance across different cohorts. Specifically, achieving a zero ROMEO score was associated with very high negative predictive value, reaching 98.6%. This suggests that for patients scoring low on this index, the likelihood of finding significant structural or functional cardiac abnormalities via TTE is quite low. Therefore, the score offers a practical method to stratify risk in the busy emergency department setting.

dailyEditorial.title: PulmCrit: Is oseltamivir assassinating ICU patients?
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Given the preliminary nature and high-risk signal from REMAP-CAP, exercise extreme caution when considering oseltamivir in critically ill patients. While further review of the full data is necessary before making definitive changes, these results strongly suggest that current institutional protocols should be reviewed to potentially de-escalate or halt routine use until more robust analyses are available.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The REMAP-CAP adaptive platform trial has released preliminary data concerning the use of oseltamivir in critically ill patients, and the findings are quite alarming.
dailyEditorial.summary: The REMAP-CAP adaptive platform trial has released preliminary data concerning the use of oseltamivir in critically ill patients, and the findings are quite alarming. The primary analysis presented suggests a substantial association between oseltamivir administration and increased mortality risk in this vulnerable population. Specifically, the reported likelihood of causing increased death was calculated at 98.3%. Given these preliminary results, clinicians should treat this data with caution but recognize that it warrants immediate attention regarding current guidelines for antiviral use in the ICU setting.

dailyEditorial.title: Shift Pearls: Anatomic Urinary Obstruction and Septic Shock
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When you suspect septic shock in a patient with potential urological compromise, remember that an obstructing process could be contributing to poor perfusion or worsening acidosis. While specific management details are pending, maintain a high index of suspicion for obstruction and consider serial urinalysis/imaging if the clinical picture is ambiguous. Be cautious about aggressive fluid resuscitation alone without addressing underlying mechanical causes.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This 'Shift Pearls' installment is flagging the critical intersection of anatomic urinary obstruction and septic shock, a common but potentially complex scenario in the ED.
dailyEditorial.summary: This 'Shift Pearls' installment is flagging the critical intersection of anatomic urinary obstruction and septic shock, a common but potentially complex scenario in the ED. While the provided excerpt only announces the topic rather than detailing specific findings or management algorithms, it signals that practical pearls are forthcoming regarding urological issues complicating sepsis. This combination demands high vigilance because acute urinary retention or obstruction can significantly worsen hemodynamic instability already present due to systemic infection. Understanding the interplay between impaired renal outflow and distributive shock is key for optimizing resuscitation efforts.

dailyEditorial.title: Case Report: When Sore Throat Is More Than Just a Pain in the Neck
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If a patient presents with persistent postoperative sore throat following intubation, don't just assume it's routine pharyngitis; perform a thorough oropharyngeal exam specifically checking the uvula for white necrotic tips. If you suspect this rare process, prompt consultation and further workup are warranted because delaying diagnosis can have serious consequences.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This case report serves as an important reminder that persistent postoperative sore throat should prompt consideration of rare, but serious, complications like uvular necrosis, rather than being dismissed as simple phar…
dailyEditorial.summary: This case report serves as an important reminder that persistent postoperative sore throat should prompt consideration of rare, but serious, complications like uvular necrosis, rather than being dismissed as simple pharyngitis. The authors emphasize that the diagnosis remains primarily clinical, necessitating a detailed history and physical examination when symptoms linger beyond 24 hours without improvement following intubation. A key diagnostic maneuver highlighted is the careful oropharyngeal inspection specifically looking for white necrotic tips on the uvula. While the mechanism linking sore throat to necrosis isn't fully elucidated here, recognizing this constellation of signs in an ED setting can prevent overlooking a significant airway complication.

dailyEditorial.title: Infant with seizure
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When evaluating infants post-trauma with concerning signs like fontanelle tension or seizures, remember that bedside cranial ultrasound can be highly diagnostic. Look for multilayered fluid collections; the absence of traversing vessels within these collections and evidence of cortical vein displacement strongly favors an SDH over SAH or hygromas. This approach helps triage imaging needs effectively.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This case report highlights the utility of bedside cranial ultrasonography in evaluating an infant presenting after trauma with concerning neurological signs, specifically irritability and tonic posturing following a fa…
dailyEditorial.summary: This case report highlights the utility of bedside cranial ultrasonography in evaluating an infant presenting after trauma with concerning neurological signs, specifically irritability and tonic posturing following a fall. The 5-month-old patient presented with physical exam findings suggestive of intracranial pathology, including ecchymoses and a tense anterior fontanelle. Ultrasound was instrumental in differentiating between potential diagnoses such as subdural hematomas (SDH), subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH), or hygromas. The characteristic appearance of bilateral, heterogeneous, multilayered collections on ultrasound, coupled with the absence of traversing vessels within these collections and evidence of displaced cortical veins, strongly supported an SDH diagnosis over other differentials.

dailyEditorial.title: ECG Cases 62 – ACLS Arrhythmia Pitfalls, Part 5: Stable Narrow Complex Tachycardias
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When faced with a stable narrow complex tachycardia, don't settle for the most obvious diagnosis; systematically rule out atrial fibrillation and flutter alongside sinus tachycardia. Always keep secondary causes in mind, as these can dictate management more than the underlying rhythm itself. Remember that accurate differentiation is key to avoiding unnecessary or harmful interventions.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This resource dives into the common pitfalls encountered when interpreting ECGs in patients presenting with stable narrow complex tachycardias, which is a frequent source of diagnostic confusion in the ED.
dailyEditorial.summary: This resource dives into the common pitfalls encountered when interpreting ECGs in patients presenting with stable narrow complex tachycardias, which is a frequent source of diagnostic confusion in the ED. The authors use eight real-world case examples to walk through the nuances required to accurately differentiate between sinus tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, and supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). Beyond just pattern recognition, the discussion stresses the critical importance of considering secondary causes for these tachyarrhythmias. Ultimately, the goal is to guide the reader toward making safe management decisions based on a thorough workup rather than relying solely on an initial rhythm strip interpretation.

dailyEditorial.title: Frailty stratification in the emergency department using the triage frailty and comorbidity tool
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Consider using the TFC tool during triage for seemingly minor complaints to proactively identify frailty risk in low-acuity patients. If positive, these individuals may benefit from earlier assessment without disrupting overall acuity flow. Remember that TFC positivity is also a marker suggesting increased downstream resource utilization like hospitalization.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This article explores integrating the Triage Frailty and Comorbidity (TFC) Tool into routine triage processes for low-acuity patients within the emergency department setting.
dailyEditorial.summary: This article explores integrating the Triage Frailty and Comorbidity (TFC) Tool into routine triage processes for low-acuity patients within the emergency department setting. The core finding suggests that incorporating this tool helps identify vulnerable, frail individuals who might otherwise be overlooked in standard acuity sorting. Specifically, its implementation was linked to reduced waiting times for medical assessment among these high-risk, low-acuity groups. Importantly, the authors noted that this improved care for the frail did not negatively impact the established system of acuity prioritization. Furthermore, TFC positivity itself correlated with a higher likelihood of subsequent hospital admission.

dailyEditorial.title: Acute urinary retention presenting as inferior pseudo-STEMI: A case report
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When you see isolated, reversible inferior ST-segment elevation in a patient who is otherwise stable and lacks clear signs of ischemia or hemodynamic instability, strongly rule out massive urinary retention. Simple catheterization can resolve the changes entirely, making this a crucial, non-cardiac differential diagnosis to consider early.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This case report details an instance where massive bladder distension presented on ECG mimicking an inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI).
dailyEditorial.summary: This case report details an instance where massive bladder distension presented on ECG mimicking an inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI). The authors highlight that the characteristic inferior ST-segment changes resolved completely following simple decompression with a Foley catheter, notably in the absence of typical hemodynamic signs like tachycardia or hypertension. This underscores that profound urinary retention can generate reversible electrical abnormalities indistinguishable from acute coronary syndrome on initial assessment. It serves as an important reminder to consider mechanical causes for ECG findings before committing to invasive cardiac workups.

dailyEditorial.title: Validation of the 2022 German trauma team activation criteria: a national registry study with focus on geriatric-specific modifiers
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing older trauma patients, remember that the 2022 TTA criteria with geriatric modifiers appear to offer superior predictive accuracy for mortality risk compared to baseline scoring. This suggests a more nuanced approach is warranted when deciding on the level of trauma resource activation. However, be aware that this validation does not quantify overtriage rates, so use these guidelines as one piece of evidence in your local resource allocation decision-making.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This national registry study validates the updated 2022 German trauma team activation (TTA) criteria, paying special attention to how geriatric-specific modifiers impact risk stratification.
dailyEditorial.summary: This national registry study validates the updated 2022 German trauma team activation (TTA) criteria, paying special attention to how geriatric-specific modifiers impact risk stratification. The authors found that incorporating these modifications significantly improves the concordance between initial triage assessments and actual observed mortality risk, which is a key metric for system performance. This improvement appears particularly pronounced within the elderly population, suggesting the updated guidelines offer better predictive power for this group compared to previous standards. While the study confirms improved alignment, it also points toward future research needing to quantify the real-world implications regarding overtriage rates and overall resource utilization across the trauma system.

dailyEditorial.title: Outcomes of Acute PE Treated With DOACs in the Veterans Affairs Health System: A Retrospective Cohort Study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For low-risk acute PE patients (sPESI 0 or 1), do not default to hospitalization if the patient is otherwise stable; outpatient management appears noninferior for 30-day mortality. Remember that this finding specifically pertains to low-risk groups, and higher sPESI scores likely warrant more aggressive inpatient consideration. This suggests a strong case for streamlining care pathways to reduce unnecessary resource utilization.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This retrospective cohort study analyzed the 30-day mortality outcomes in a large U.S.
dailyEditorial.summary: This retrospective cohort study analyzed the 30-day mortality outcomes in a large U.S. population presenting with acute pulmonary embolism (PE) who were managed with direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs). The authors stratified their analysis based on the Wells' criteria equivalent, using the sPESI score to categorize risk. Notably, for patients classified as low-risk, specifically those with an sPESI of 0 or 1, the study found that undergoing hospitalization did not confer a survival benefit compared to being managed in the outpatient setting. Given that two-thirds of these low-risk patients were still admitted despite this finding, the results strongly advocate for reevaluating the necessity of short-stay hospitalizations when no other compelling admission criteria are present.

dailyEditorial.title: No Pause, No Problem? Using Doppler Ultrasound to Detect ROSC Without Pausing Compressions for Pulse Check
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If you are in a setting where Doppler ultrasound is readily available, consider its utility to assess for ROSC without interrupting compressions for pulse checks. The ability to maintain continuous chest compression quality by using flow signals warrants consideration, but remember that this remains an evaluation of diagnostic accuracy and should not replace established ACLS algorithms or clinical judgment.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review tackles the persistent issue of interruptions during cardiac arrest care, specifically focusing on how routine pulse checks disrupt high-quality CPR efforts.
dailyEditorial.summary: This review tackles the persistent issue of interruptions during cardiac arrest care, specifically focusing on how routine pulse checks disrupt high-quality CPR efforts. The core concept explored is leveraging femoral arterial Doppler ultrasound waveforms obtained *during* active compressions to detect signs of Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC). Instead of pausing chest compressions every two minutes for a traditional pulse check, the authors evaluate if detecting pulsatility and anterograde flow signals can reliably predict true cardiac activity. If validated, this represents a significant workflow improvement by maintaining continuous, uninterrupted high-quality CPR monitoring, which is paramount for optimizing resuscitation outcomes.

dailyEditorial.title: Patterns of emergency department visits for suicidal attempts associated with toxic ingestion: A retrospective cross-sectional study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing a patient presenting with intentional overdose in the ED, anticipate polypharmacy involving CNS depressants as common agents. Given this complexity, prompt toxicology consultation is non-negotiable for guiding antidotal therapy and monitoring for multi-system effects. Remember that optimizing care involves coordinating both acute stabilization and risk assessment.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This retrospective analysis characterizing intentional self-poisoning presentations in Israel highlights a pattern where medication overdose is the most common etiology, frequently involving polypharmacy, particularly w…
dailyEditorial.summary: This retrospective analysis characterizing intentional self-poisoning presentations in Israel highlights a pattern where medication overdose is the most common etiology, frequently involving polypharmacy, particularly with central nervous system depressants. The authors emphasize that managing these complex ingestions requires more than just standard clinical care; early and robust toxicology consultation is crucial for guiding definitive treatment pathways and improving patient outcomes. Furthermore, the report underscores the broader role of medical toxicologists, extending their purview beyond immediate stabilization to include surveillance, prevention efforts, and addressing the psychological aspects of self-harm risk.

dailyEditorial.title: Status epilepticus in patients with known epilepsy presenting to the emergency department: Predictors and mortality risk
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Given the significant association between SE in this population and increased mortality, consider systematically assessing all incoming epilepsy patients for underlying triggers. Pay extra attention to those who are elderly, have known metabolic issues, or present with signs suggestive of CNS infection, as these groups warrant heightened suspicion for impending or ongoing seizures. Early risk stratification is key here.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review looked at the prevalence and predictors of status epilepticus (SE) specifically within a cohort of epilepsy patients presenting to the emergency department in Malaysia.
dailyEditorial.summary: This review looked at the prevalence and predictors of status epilepticus (SE) specifically within a cohort of epilepsy patients presenting to the emergency department in Malaysia. The authors found that SE was quite common, occurring in nearly one-quarter of this population, and critically, it was strongly associated with elevated in-hospital mortality rates. This suggests that simply having an epilepsy diagnosis does not preclude the risk of severe status epilepticus upon presentation. The findings emphasize the utility of identifying high-risk subgroups for proactive management. Specifically, the data point toward older age, metabolic derangements, or suspicion of a central nervous system infection as key areas for enhanced vigilance and early risk stratification.

dailyEditorial.title: Risk of obstructive acute kidney injury: derivation and internal validation of a risk stratification tree
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing AKI in the ED, consider using this framework to triage suspicion for obstruction; acute flank/lumbar pain strongly suggests a higher pre-test probability. Remember that while the model shows high sensitivity for 'low risk,' it is based on internal validation and requires external testing before changing standard diagnostic pathways. Clinicians should interpret these scores as adjuncts to physical exam findings, not replacements.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This paper introduces the KIT-FISTO model, a newly developed risk stratification decision tree designed to predict obstructive acute kidney injury (AKI) in the emergency department setting.
dailyEditorial.summary: This paper introduces the KIT-FISTO model, a newly developed risk stratification decision tree designed to predict obstructive acute kidney injury (AKI) in the emergency department setting. The study utilized a retrospective derivation and internal validation cohort approach on adult patients presenting with AKI of any KDIGO stage. The model stratifies risk based on clinical presentation; specifically, patients reporting lumbar, flank, or hypogastric pain are categorized as 'high risk,' showing an associated obstructive AKI risk around 55%. A 'moderate risk' group is defined for those without acute pain but with specific histories such as prior urinary tract surgery or abdominal cancer. The authors note that the model achieved high sensitivity for the 'low risk' category, suggesting its utility in ruling out significant obstruction.

dailyEditorial.title: Real-time identification of aetiology in patients able to undergo transoesophageal echocardiography with non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest in China: a prospective, single-centre exploratory study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: TEE appears to be a rapid and feasible tool for identifying suspected causes during CPR in NT-OHCA patients, with acute aortic dissection being a key finding. However, remember that this data comes from a single center without a control group, so do not change your standard resuscitation algorithm based on this alone. It remains a valuable adjunct for guiding immediate interventions when available.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This single-center, prospective study explored the utility of transoesophageal echocardiography (TEE) for identifying reversible causes in patients who experienced non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (NT-OHCA)…
dailyEditorial.summary: This single-center, prospective study explored the utility of transoesophageal echocardiography (TEE) for identifying reversible causes in patients who experienced non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (NT-OHCA) and were able to undergo the procedure. The authors reported that TEE successfully provided diagnostic images in all 43 eligible patients examined, with a median door-to-TEE time of 13 minutes. They found that reversible etiologies were identified in 39% of cases, with acute aortic dissection being the most frequently diagnosed condition at 50%. While TEE guided interventions in over half of the identified cases, the study noted that there was no significant difference in both return of spontaneous circulation or 28-day survival rates when comparing patients whose cause was identified versus those where it was not. Crucially, the authors cautioned that due to the lack of a comparator group, further controlled investigation is necessary before drawing definitive conclusions about its role.
